title: PROGRESS
text: What have I been doing in the last week or two? I downloaded the Protege desktop app from https://protege.stanford.edu and imported the ICD-10 PCS ontology from BioPortal. I was a little worried about installing the desktop app because I saw that some users were have difficulty installing it on Mac. I tried using the Protege web app, but it doesn’t appear to have a tab for writing SPARQL queries against an ontology. There’s some discussion online about the web app’s functionality; users are wondering why the Protege web app doesn’t support SPARQL; or, if it does, how to use it. At least the desktop app does have a pane for writing SPARQL queries, and I was able to install Protege without much trouble, so I’ll be able to move onto the SPARQL portion of my project

title: WHAT OWLS KNOW
text:
We’re told that owls are the most wise of all birds. As children, we’re told to revere the wisdom of these bespectacled, nocturnal, hunters but without any evidence that they’re smarter than, say, a… woodcock, a bird that I think appears equally perceptive. But let’s suspend disbelief and assume that owls are the wisest of the bird kingdom. Then, let’s suspend disbelief again and imagine that owls can find employment as computer scientists or informaticists.
Being wise as they are, owls would certainly endorse a codified, international, standard for describing domain-specific knowledge. Maybe it’s no coincidence that OWL has become W3C’s standard language for authoring an ontology: a structured description of concepts, and the relationship between them, within some domain of knowledge.
OWL became W3C’s semantic language of choice in 2004, over 20 years ago, not long after Tim Berners-Lee proposed the so-called Semantic Web as the rebirth of the World Wide Web. Berners-Lee imagined ripping out the web’s tangled mess of hyperlinks and replacing them all with a systematic, interconnected, web of datapoints described, and separated by, the relationships between themselves. Unlike the World Wide Web, the Semantic Web would be machine-readable and allow for more automated processing.
It never happened. Nobody wanted to remake their website with semantic web standards. Then LLMs were unleashed on the world. After being trained on millions and millions of documents scraped from the web, they “learned” how to read information from the unstructured, supposedly unreadable, World Wide Web.
So why do we still care about semantic data architecture? Learning OWL sounds about as useful as learning a dead language like Latin.
LLMs, despite hastening the early death of the Semantic Web, benefit from a little structured data on top of the massive ocean of unstructured data that feeds their abilities. This so-called semantic layer refines their output, adds precision to their responses, and prevents hallucinations. Semantic descriptions of data provide domain-specific context that allow AI tools to generate consistent, business-friendly outputs.
LLMs might even help us with some of the drudgery that prevented the realization of Tim Berger-Lee’s semantic web.
Not even an owl could’ve seen that coming.

title: HELLO WORLD!
text: Join me as I explore tools for semantic data.

title: GETTING TO KNOW THE SEMANTIC WEB
text: I’m wrapping up this week by watching some introductory videos about the semantic web and semantic web standards, particularly OWL. Semantic web technologies aren’t covered in depth in any undergraduate computer science course (at least not at the University of Colorado), so I began by looking for some approachable, hopefully free, resources that don’t assume much domain-specific knowledge. There aren’t many free options available! Udemy offers a comprehensive MOOC that covers OWL, RDF and even SPARQL (https://www.udemy.com/course/semanticweb/), but it costs $120 - too much for now, but maybe I’ll come back to it. Instead, I’m watching following along with this program from the Hasso Plattner Institute in Germany: https://open.hpi.de/courses/semanticweb. Next, I’ll look for some videos that more specifically cover OWL standards. It looks like Stanford’s Protégé team hosts “Protégé Short Courses,” but I can’t access it; it looks like the Protégé website is actually down this evening! Hopefully it’s fixed before I attempt to install Protégé…

title: Ontology Development: Part 1
text:
Before I begin querying an existing ontology like those on BioPortal, I’ll attempt to develop my own ontology. I’ll loosely follow the Protégé guide to ontology development, Ontology Development 101: A Guide to Creating Your First Ontology, by Noy and McGuinness (2001):
https://protege.stanford.edu/publications/ontology_development/ontology101.pdf
There are a handful of reasons why we create ontologies, but most importantly, we create ontologies to share our understanding of domain-specific knowledge.
I’ll develop an ontology specific to my domain of career expertise: health quality measurement. More specifically, I’ll develop an ontology that describes components of the HEDIS quality metrics published by the National Committee for Quality Assurance (NCQA).
If you want to see the full set of HEDIS measures and how they’re defined, you’ll need to purchase a copy of HEDIS Volume 2 and its technical specifications. Instead, you can review a summary of HEDIS measures used by Johns Hopkins Health Plans:
https://www.hopkinsmedicine.org/johns-hopkins-health-plans/providers-physicians/health-care-performance-measures/hedis

title: Ontology Development: Part 1
text:
Before I begin querying an existing ontology like those on BioPortal, I’ll attempt to develop my own ontology. I’ll loosely follow the Protégé guide to ontology development, Ontology Development 101: A Guide to Creating Your First Ontology, by Noy and McGuinness (2001):
https://protege.stanford.edu/publications/ontology_development/ontology101.pdf
There are a handful of reasons why we create ontologies, but most importantly, we create ontologies to share our understanding of domain-specific knowledge.
I’ll develop an ontology specific to my domain of career expertise: health quality measurement. More specifically, I’ll develop an ontology that describes components of the HEDIS quality metrics published by the National Committee for Quality Assurance (NCQA).
If you want to see the full set of HEDIS measures and how they’re defined, you’ll need to purchase a copy of HEDIS Volume 2 and its technical specifications. Instead, you can review a summary of HEDIS measures used by Johns Hopkins Health Plans:
https://www.hopkinsmedicine.org/johns-hopkins-health-plans/providers-physicians/health-care-performance-measures/hedis

title: Ontology Development: Part 1
section: Step 1: Determine the Domain and Scope of the Ontology
text:
Step 1: Determine the Domain and Scope of the Ontology
Noy and McGuinness suggest answering the following foundational questions:

What is the domain that the ontology will cover?

How will the ontology be used?

What questions will the ontology be able to answer?

Who will use and maintain the ontology?


Answers

Domain
My ontology will focus on healthcare quality measurement. I will begin with HEDIS measures but may expand to include additional measurement frameworks in the future.
Usage
It will be used by individuals or organizations that measure healthcare quality (e.g., health plans) to better understand the components of HEDIS measures, such as what data is used to calculate a measure.
Questions the Ontology Should Answer
The ontology should help answer questions about:

What health statuses are addressed by HEDIS measures

The degree of overlap between measures

Example question:
Do two HEDIS measures use nearly identical diagnostic codes?
Maintenance
I do not currently plan to distribute this ontology. However, if I did, I would be responsible for maintaining it.

title: Ontology Development: Part 1
text:
Competency Questions
The ontology should be able to answer competency questions used to evaluate its completeness. If it cannot answer them, additional refinement is needed.
Initial competency question:

How much overlap is there between two HEDIS measures?

title: Ontology Development: Part 1
text:
Step 2: Consider Reusing Existing Ontologies
There isn’t one.
Next…

title: Ontology Development: Part 1
section: Step 3: Enumerate Important Terms in the Ontology
text:
Step 3: Enumerate Important Terms in the Ontology
Now, I will list the key terms that should be included in the ontology.
Noy and McGuinness use a wine ontology example with terms like grape, winery, and location.
For my ontology, initial terms include:

Product line (e.g., Medicaid)

Value sets

Codes

Code systems

Medications

This list will likely expand as development progresses.

title: Ontology Development: Part 1
text:
Next Steps
In Part 2, I will begin defining classes and class hierarchies.

title: Joshua Morrison
text:
I’m Joshua Morrison, a Data Scientist and public health informatics professional. I use data from medical claims and electronic health records to inform healthcare quality measurement, especially for patients with chronic conditions. I’m also an aspiring semantic data specialist with an eye toward healthcare interoperability and AI applications. I’ll be using this site to chronicle my self-led study of semantic data tools.
